prompt:
What are the treatments for Infantile Refsum Disease?
A. Avoidance of foods rich in phytanic acid
B. Antioxidant therapy
C. Liver transplantation
D. Enzyme replacement therapy
completion:
The primary treatment for IRD is to avoid foods that contain phytanic acid, including dairy products; beef and lamb; and fatty fish such as tuna, cod, and haddock. Although this prevents the accumulation of phytanic acid, it does not correct the metabolic problems of IRD. However, liver transplantation has been used as a last resort for IRD with severe neurological impairment or end-stage liver disease. Enzyme replacement therapy has been used to treat a type of IRD called aryl-sulfatase A deficiency.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
What causes Langerhans cell histiocytosis?
A. An infection
B. A tumor
C. An autoimmune disorder
D. An allergy
completion:
The cause of Langerhans cell histiocytosis is unknown. It may be triggered by an unusual reaction of the immune system to something commonly found in the environment. It is not considered to be an infection or cancer. It is not known to be hereditary or communicable.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease?
A. None
B. Plasmapheresis
C. Bone marrow transplant
D. None of these
completion:
The treatments for Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease include corticosteroids and anticonvulsants.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Aicardi Syndrome?
A. Research on treatments for seizures
B. Research on the development of the nervous system
C. Research on the inheritance of Aicardi syndrome
D. Research on the relationship between Aicardi syndrome and other genetic disorders
completion:
The NINDS supports and conducts research on neurogenetic disorders such as Aicardi syndrome. The goals of this research are to locate and understand the genes involved and to develop techniques to diagnose, treat, prevent, and ultimately cure disorders like Aicardi syndrome.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
Is Bloom syndrome inherited?
A. It is inherited.
B. It is not inherited.
C. It is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
D. It is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.
completion:
Bloom syndrome is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means both copies of the gene in each cell have mutations. The parents of an individual with an autosomal recessive condition each carry one copy of the mutated gene, but they typically do not show signs and symptoms of the condition.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Northern epilepsy?
A. Surgery
B. Medication
C. Diet
D. None of the above
completion:
The treatments for Northern epilepsy include medication and a special diet.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
How many people are affected by renal tubular acidosis with deafness?
A. 5 million people
B. 10 million people
C. 15 million people
D. 20 million people
completion:
Renal tubular acidosis with deafness is a rare disorder; its prevalence is unknown.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Kennedy's Disease?
A. A surgical procedure
B. Medication
C. A lifestyle change
D. None of the above
completion:
Currently there is no known cure for Kennedy's disease. Treatment is symptomatic and supportive.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
Is Chronic hiccups inherited?
A. A genetic disorder
B. An infectious disease
C. A metabolic disorder
D. A neurological disorder
completion:
Chronic hiccups are not thought to be inherited. Most cases occur sporadically in people with no family history of the condition.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for tyrosine hydroxylase deficiency?
A. Gene therapy
B. Surgery
C. Palliative care
D. Dietary changes
completion:
There are no curative treatments for tyrosine hydroxylase deficiency; the mainstay of therapy is palliative care.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
Is Asperger syndrome inherited?
A. Yes
B. No
C. Yes, but only in families with a history of autism spectrum disorders
D. No, it's a sporadic condition
completion:
Autism spectrum disorders including Asperger syndrome have a tendency to run in families, but the inheritance pattern is unknown.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
Is Baraitser-Winter syndrome inherited?
A. Autosomal dominant
B. Autosomal recessive
C. X-linked dominant
D. X-linked recessive
completion:
This condition is described as autosomal dominant, which means one copy of the altered gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the disorder. The condition almost always results from new (de novo) mutations in the ACTB or ACTG1 gene and occurs in people with no history of the disorder in their family.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for VIPoma?
A. A special diet
B. Surgery to remove the tumor
C. Medications to control diarrhea
D. Intravenous fluids to correct dehydration
completion:
Treatment for VIPoma may include intravenous (IV) fluids to correct dehydration, medications such as octreotide to help control diarrhea, and surgery to remove the tumor. If the tumor has spread (metastasized) to the liver, a liver transplant may be necessary.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
Is hereditary sensory and autonomic neuropathy type IE inherited?
A. Yes
B. No
C. Autosomal recessive
D. Autosomal dominant
completion:
This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, which means one copy of the altered gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the disorder. In most cases, an affected person has one parent with the condition.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Human T-cell leukemia virus type 1?
A. Blood transfusions
B. Chemotherapy
C. Radiation therapy
D. Surgery
completion:
No cure or treatment exists for human T-cell leukemia virus, type 1 (HTLV-1). Management is focused on early detection and preventing the spread of HTLV-1 to others.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Machado-Joseph Disease?
A. A skin condition
  B. A viral infection
  C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
  D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Lyme disease is treated with antibiotics under the supervision of a physician.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Gum (Periodontal) Disease?
A. tooth decay
B. gingivitis
C. periodontitis
D. abscesses
completion:
Gum disease is the leading cause of tooth loss in older adults. If left untreated, gum disease can lead to tooth loss.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Ohtahara Syndrome?
A. Antiepileptic drugs
B. Corticosteroids
C. Surgery
D. None of these
completion:
Antiepileptic drugs are used to control seizures, but are unfortunately not usually very effective for this disorder. Corticosteroids are occasionally helpful. In cases where there is a focal brain lesion (damage contained to one area of the brain) surgery may be performed to remove the lesion.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:

This condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means both copies of the gene in each cell have mutations. prompt:

prompt:
How many people are affected by Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome?

    D. 10,000

prompt:

completion:

prompt:
How many people are affected by Anxiety Disorders?

B. 15 million
C. 150 million
D. 200 million
completion:
Studies estimate that anxiety disorders affect around 15 percent of older adults in a given year.

prompt:

completion:
There is no standard course of treatment for holoprosencephaly. Treatment is symptomatic and supportive.

D. A lifestyle change
completion:
The only known treatments for 3-hydroxyisobutyric aciduria are a protein-restricted diet and carnitine supplements.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Alexander Disease?
A. It is a lifelong, progressive disorder.
  B. It is a fatal disease that generally affects children under the age of 6.
  C. It is a rare disease that is typically fatal.
  D. It is a chronic, debilitating disease.
completion:
Most children with the infantile form do not survive past the age of 6. Juvenile and adult onset forms of the disorder have a slower, more lengthy course.

prompt:
what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Multi-Infarct Dementia?

completion:

D. Maybe
completion:
This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, which means one copy of the altered gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the disorder. Almost all cases result from new mutations in the gene and occur in people with no history of the disorder in their family.

prompt:
What are the treatments for Shwachman-Diamond syndrome?

B. Multivitamin supplements

Treatment of SBDS is focused on treating the symptoms and complications of the syndrome. Treatment may include: - Pancreatic enzyme supplements to help with digestion - Multivitamin supplements - Antibiotics to treat infections - Diarrhea treatments
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
Is Renal oncocytoma inherited?
A. Yes, renal oncocytoma is inherited.
B. No, renal oncocytoma is not inherited.
C. Both A and B.

prompt:

A. A viral infection
B. A skin condition
C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Cerebrotendinous xanthomatosis is a type of lipid storage disease. Symptoms of this condition include diarrhea in infants, cataracts in children, tendon xanthomas, and progressive neurologic dysfunction.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Dravet Syndrome?
A. Skin studies
B. Viral studies

The NINDS conducts and supports a broad program of basic and clinical research on all types of epilepsy, including Dravet syndrome. Study of the genetic defects responsible for Dravet syndrome and related disorders is expected to lead to more effective treatments.

prompt:

completion:
The outlook for Periventricular Leukomalacia depends upon the severity of the brain damage. Some children have mild symptoms, while others have significant deficits and disabilities. The outlook can be good, with full recovery expected, or poor, with little or no recovery.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Repetitive Motion Disorders?
A. The outlook for RMDs is good, and most people recover completely.
B. Most people recover, but some experience permanent joint injury.
C. Permanent joint injury is a common outcome without treatment.
D. Treatment is rarely necessary.
completion:
Most individuals with RMDs recover completely and can avoid re-injury by changing the way they perform repetitive movements, the frequency with which they perform them, and the amount of time they rest between movements. Without treatment, RMDs may result in permanent injury and complete loss of function in the affected joint.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:

prompt:
What is the outlook for Myotonia Congenita?
A. Death is common
B. Severe disability
C. Can be cured
D. Symptoms go away over time
completion:
Most individuals with myotonia congenita lead long, productive lives. Although muscle stiffness may interfere with walking, grasping, chewing, and swallowing, it is usually relieved with exercise.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
Who is at risk for Prostate Cancer??
A. African American men
B. Asian men
C. White men
D. Having a family history of prostate cancer
completion:
Age is the most important risk factor for prostate cancer. The disease is extremely rare in men under age 40, but the risk increases greatly with age. More than 60 percent of cases are diagnosed in men over age 65. The average age at the time of diagnosis is 65.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What is (are) High Blood Cholesterol?
A. A type of heart failure
B. A skin condition
C. A viral infection
D. A type of cancer
completion:
Your LDL goal is how low your LDL cholesterol level should be to reduce your risk of developing heart disease or having a heart attack. The higher your risk, the lower your goal LDL should be. Your doctor will set your LDL goal using your medical history and the number of risk factors that you have for heart disease.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Cerebellar Hypoplasia?
A. Surgery
B. Medication
C. Physical therapy
D. None of the above
completion:

prompt:
What are the treatments for What I need to know about Cirrhosis?
A. Surgery to remove the damaged liver lobe
B. Liver transplant
C. Medicines that make the liver less acidic
D. Liver dialysis
completion:
If too much scar tissue forms, your liver could fail. Then you will need a liver transplant. A liver transplant can return you to good health. For information about liver transplantation, see the booklet What I need to know about Liver Transplantation.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What is (are) Gay, Lesbian, Bisexual and Transgender Health?
A. Skin conditions
B. Viral infections

completion:

completion:

prompt:
What is the outlook for Congenital Myopathy?
A. A poor outlook
B. A good outlook
C. A fair outlook
D. A good outlook with treatment
completion:
When breathing difficulties are severe, and particularly if there is also a problem with feeding and swallowing, infants may die of respiratory failure or complications such as pneumonia. Sometimes muscle weakness can lead to skeletal problems, such as scoliosis, reduced chest development and delayed walking.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Inclusion Body Myositis?
A. Variable
B. Poorly understood
C. Resistant to all therapies
D. Variable
completion:
IBM is generally resistant to all therapies and its rate of progression appears to be unaffected by currently available treatments.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What is (are) Leber hereditary optic neuropathy?
A. A skin condition
B. A viral infection
C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON) is an inherited form of vision loss. Although this condition usually begins in a person's teens or twenties, rare cases may appear in early childhood or later in adulthood. For unknown reasons, boys are more often affected than girls.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Myasthenia Gravis?
A. Death is a common outcome
B. The outlook is poor
C. Symptoms improve with treatment
D. Without treatment, symptoms will worsen over time
completion:
With treatment, most individuals with myasthenia can significantly improve their muscle weakness. Some case of myasthenia gravis may go into remission temporarily, and muscle weakness may disappear so that medications can be discontinued. In a few cases, the severe weakness of myasthenia gravis may lead to permanent disability.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:

prompt:
What are the treatments for Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy?

B. If there are high levels of phosphate in the blood, it may be recommended to eat a low-phosphorous diet or take medications that reduce phosphate levels.
C. Surgery is not recommended.
D. There is no known method for prevention.
completion:
Treatment with calcium and vitamin D supplements help maintain normal levels of calcium in the blood. If there are high levels of phosphate in the blood, it may be recommended to eat a low-phosphorous diet or take medications that reduce phosphate levels. Surgery is not recommended. There is no known method for prevention.

prompt:

    B. A viral infection
    C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
    D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Myasthenia gravis affects about 20 per 100,000 people worldwide. The prevalence has been increasing in recent decades, which likely results from earlier diagnosis and better treatments leading to longer lifespans for affected individuals.

prompt:
What are the treatments for Isaacs' Syndrome?
A. Botulinum toxin injections
B. Anticonvulsants
C. Plasma exchange
D. β-blockers
completion:
Anticonvulsants, including phenytoin and carbamazepine, usually provide significant relief from the stiffness, muscle spasms, and pain associated with Isaacs' syndrome. Plasma exchange may provide short-term relief for individuals with some forms of the acquired disorder. β-blockers, such as nadolol and propranolol, are used in the treatment of Isaacs' syndrome.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:

D. 1 people

<answer>B</answer>

This condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means both copies of the gene in each cell have mutations. <answer>D</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Benign Essential Blepharospasm?
A. In most cases of BEB the treatment of choice is botulinum toxin injections which relax the muscles and stop the spasms.
B. Medications (drug therapy) or surgery--either local surgery of the eye muscles or deep brain stimulation (DBS) may be used to treat BEB.
C. Botulinum toxin injections are not the treatment of choice for BEB.
D. Surgery is not the treatment of choice for BEB.
completion:
In most cases of BEB the treatment of choice is botulinum toxin injections which relax the muscles and stop the spasms. Other treatment options include medications (drug therapy) or surgery--either local surgery of the eye muscles or deep brain stimulation (DBS).
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
How many people are affected by Mabry syndrome?
A. 100,000
B. 1,000,000
C. 10,000,000
D. 20
completion:
The prevalence of Mabry syndrome is unknown, but more than 20 cases have been described in the scientific literature.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Essential Thrombocythemia?
Good prognosis
High chance of recovery.
A. Young age
B. No signs or symptoms
C. No other problems from the disease
D. No leg bleeding or other bleeding
Poor prognosis
Low chance of recovery.
A. Older age
B. Signs or symptoms
C. Other problems from the disease
D. Leg bleeding or other bleeding
completion:
Prognosis (chance of recovery) and treatment options depend on the age of the patient, signs or symptoms, and bleeding problems. Patients who are young, with no signs or symptoms, and no bleeding have a good prognosis.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
How many people are affected by Lesch-Nyhan syndrome?
A. 1 in 380,000 individuals
B. 1 in 200,000 people
C. 1 in 100,000 people
D. 1 in 50,000 people
completion:
The prevalence of Lesch-Nyhan syndrome is approximately 1 in 380,000 individuals. This condition occurs with a similar frequency in all populations. in the US.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
how can y. enterocolitica infections be treated for Yersinia?
A. No treatment needed
B. Antibiotics such as aminoglycosides, doxycycline, or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole may be used.
C. Complicated cases may require intravenous antibiotics.
D. Contact your healthcare provider immediately if you experience symptoms of a Yersinia infection.
completion:
Antibiotics such as aminoglycosides, doxycycline, or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole may be used to treat Y. enterocolitica infections. However, uncomplicated cases of diarrhea due to Y. enterocolitica usually resolve on their own without antibiotic treatment. If you experience symptoms of a Yersinia infection, contact your healthcare provider immediately.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Kleine-Levin Syndrome?
A. A skin condition
B. A viral infection
C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Kleine-Levin Syndrome is a rare disorder that causes recurrent episodes of confusion, memory loss, and other symptoms.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
How to diagnose Glucose transporter type 1 deficiency syndrome?
A. Skin biopsy
B. Blood test
C. MRI brain
D. EEG
completion:
A blood test can confirm the diagnosis by detecting mutations in the SLC2A1 gene. MRI of the brain is used to evaluate the brain for signs of glucose accumulation.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
Is Troyer syndrome inherited?
A. Yes
B. No
C. Autosomal dominant
D. Autosomal recessive
completion:
This condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means both copies of the gene in each cell have mutations. The parents of an individual with an autosomal recessive condition each carry one copy of the mutated gene, but they typically do not show signs and symptoms of the condition.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Gerstmann-Straussler-Scheinker Disease?
A. Recovery
B. Good prognosis
C. Poor prognosis
D. Recovery with treatment
completion:
GSS is a slowly progressive condition usually lasting from 2 to 10 years. The disease ultimately causes severe disability and finally death, often after the patient goes into a coma or has a secondary infection such as aspiration pneumonia.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Tourette Syndrome?
A. It will affect every aspect of their life and lead to early death.
B. It will never go away.
C. It will get progressively worse forever.
D. The symptoms are temporary and will go away as they grow older.
completion:
Although TS can be a chronic condition with symptoms lasting a lifetime, most people with the condition experience their worst symptoms in their early teens, with improvement occurring in the late teens and continuing into adulthood. As a result, some individuals may actually become symptom-free as they get older.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
How many people are affected by short QT syndrome?
A. A type of heart failure
B. A lung disease
C. A skin condition
D. A viral infection
completion:
Short QT syndrome appears to be rare. At least 70 cases have been identified worldwide since the condition was discovered in 2000. However, the condition may be underdiagnosed because some affected individuals never experience symptoms. QT syndrome is more common in women than in men.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Gaucher Disease?
A. The outlook for Gaucher Disease is good.
B. Enzyme replacement therapy is very beneficial for type 1 and most type 3 individuals with this condition.
C. The outlook for Gaucher Disease is good.
D. Successful bone marrow transplantation can reverse the non-neurological effects of the disease, but the procedure carries a high risk and is rarely performed in individuals with Gaucher disease.
completion:
Enzyme replacement therapy is very beneficial for type 1 and most type 3 individuals with this condition.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
How many people are affected by atypical hemolytic-uremic syndrome?
C. 1 in 500,000 people per year
B. 100,000 cases per year
D. 50,000 cases per year
A. 200,000 cases per year
completion:
The incidence of atypical hemolytic-uremic syndrome is estimated to be 1 in 500,000 people per year in the United States.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Dentatorubral-pallidoluysian atrophy?
A. Surgery
B. Physical therapy
C. Antibiotics
D. Antivirals
completion:
There is no cure for DRPLA; treatment is generally symptomatic and supportive. Management of signs and symptoms may include: Treatment of seizures with anti-epileptic drugs Treatment of psychiatric problems with appropriate medications Pain management Speech therapy to prevent or correct muscle weakness affecting swallowing and speaking
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Schilder's Disease?
A. Positive
B. Good
C. Poor
D. Unpredictable
completion:
The course and prognosis of Schilder's disease are unpredictable. For some individuals the disorder is progressive with a steady, unremitting course. Others may experience significant improvement and even remission. In some cases, Schilder's disease may transform into multiple sclerosis.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
Is medullary cystic kidney disease type 1 inherited?
A. A skin condition
B. A viral infection
C. A kidney disease
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, which means one copy of the altered gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the disorder.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What is (are) Lymphomatoid papulosis?
A. A skin condition
B. A type of cancer
C. A parasitic infection
D. A viral infection
completion:
Lymphomatoid papulosis is a skin disorder that is characterized by crops of self healing skin lesions that look cancerous under the microscope but are actually benign (non-cancerous). Lesions contain unusual cells that are similar to those found in lymphoma and hairy cell leukemia.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Locked-In Syndrome?
A. Locked-In Syndrome is a permanent vegetative state.
B. There is no cure for Locked-In Syndrome.
C. A therapy called functional neuromuscular stimulation may activate some paralyzed muscles.
D. Devices to help with communication are available. These include a head-mouse and a Brain-Computer Interface (BCI).
completion:
The treatments for Locked-In Syndrome include a therapy called functional neuromuscular stimulation, which may activate some paralyzed muscles.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What is the outlook for Metachromatic Leukodystrophy?
A. Good
B. Poor
C. Fair
D. None of these
completion:
The prognosis for MLD is poor. Most children within the infantile form die by age 5. Symptoms of the juvenile form progress with death occurring 10 to 20 years following onset. Those persons affected by the adult form typically die with the disease.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Hydranencephaly?
A. Surgery
B. Antiepileptic medications
C. Developmental delays treated with physical and occupational therapy
D. Seizures treated with surgery
completion:
Hydranencephaly is a condition with no definitive treatment. Management focuses on the specific symptoms present in the affected individual. Antiepileptic medications may treat seizures, shunt placement may treat hydrocephalus, and physical and occupational therapy may treat developmental delays.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What is (are) Coccygodynia?
A. A skin condition
B. A viral infection
C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Coccygodynia is a rare condition in that causes pain in and around the coccyx (tailbone). Although various causes have been described for the condition, the more common causes are direct falls and injury.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What causes Meesmann corneal dystrophy?
A. An infection
B. A skin condition
C. A viral infection
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Meesmann corneal dystrophy is a genetic disease. It can be caused by mutations in either the KRT12 or KRT3 gene. These genes are thought to play an important role in maintaining normal corneal epithelial function. Meesmann corneal dystrophy is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means two copies of the faulty gene in each of your parents are required for the disorder to be manifested.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
Is Chorea-acanthocytosis inherited?
A. Yes
B. No
C. Autosomal dominant
D. Autosomal recessive
completion:
Chorea-acanthocytosis is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means both copies of the gene in each cell have mutations. The parents of an individual with an autosomal recessive condition each carry one copy of the mutated gene, but they typically do not show signs and symptoms of the condition.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Lesch-Nyhan Syndrome?
A. Researching a cure
B. Researching a way to prevent it
C. Researching a way to diagnose it
D. Researching a way to treat it
completion:
The NINDS supports and conducts research on genetic disorders such as LNS in an effort to find ways to prevent and treat these disorders.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
Is Kawasaki syndrome inherited?
A. A genetic defect
B. An environmental toxin
C. A virus
D. A combination of genetic and environmental factors
completion:
The cause of Kawasaki syndrome is unknown, but it is not believed to be inherited. It is likely a combination of genetic and environmental factors.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Aphasia?
A. A skin condition
B. A viral infection
C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke and the National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders conduct and support a broad range of scientific investigations to increase our understanding of aphasia, find better treatments, and discover improved methods to restore lost function.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Hypomyelination with atrophy of basal ganglia and cerebellum?
A. Physical therapy
B. Chemical/electrical stimulation
C. Gene therapy
D. Bone marrow transplant
completion:
There is no known cure for hypomyelination with atrophy of basal ganglia and cerebellum (H-ABC). However, a bone marrow transplant has been shown to improve one patient's movement difficulties.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
Is multiple mitochondrial dysfunctions syndrome inherited?
A. Yes
B. No
C. Yes, it is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern
D. No, it is not inherited
completion:
This condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means both copies of the gene in each cell have mutations. The parents of an individual with an autosomal recessive condition each carry one copy of the mutated gene, but they typically do not show signs and symptoms of the condition.

prompt:
What are the treatments for Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome?
A. Thiamine replacement
B. Nutritional therapy
C. Drug therapy
D. Stopping alcohol use
completion:
Treatment involves replacement of thiamine and providing proper nutrition and hydration. In some cases, drug therapy is also recommended.Stopping alcohol use may prevent further nerve and brain damage. In individuals with Wernicke's encephalopathy, thiamine replacement and nutritional therapy may be enough to prevent the development of Korsakoff syndrome.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What causes Punctate palmoplantar keratoderma type I?
A. A skin condition
B. A viral infection
C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Punctate palmoplantar keratoderma type 1 is a condition that is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant manner. It has recently been shown to be caused by mutations in the AAGAB gene in several families. Although the exact function of the AAGAB gene is not known, it is suspected to be involved in keratin synthesis.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for hyperferritinemia-cataract syndrome?
A. Surgery
B. Medication
C. Laser eye surgery
D. Regular eye check-ups
completion:
A. Surgery
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
Is keratoderma with woolly hair inherited?

completion:

<answer>B</answer>

prompt:

completion:
This condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means both copies of the gene in each cell have mutations. prompt:
How many people are affected by bladder cancer?

  B. 100,000
  C. 50,000
  D. 10,000
completion:
Bladder cancer is common in the United States. About 45,000 men and 17,000 women are diagnosed with bladder cancer each year.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:
What causes Amniotic band syndrome?
A. A genetic disorder
B. A birth defect
C. An infection
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Amniotic bands are caused by damage to a part of the placenta called the amnion. Damage to the amnion may produce fiber-like bands that can trap parts of the developing baby.
<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
What causes Psoriasis?
A. A skin condition
B. A viral infection
C. A lung disease that causes airway inflammation
D. A type of heart failure
completion:
Although the cause of psoriasis is not completely understood, scientists believe it is related to a problem with a type of blood cells called T cells. These cells normally travel through the bloodstream to help fight an infection, but in people with psoriasis, they attack the bodys skin cells by mistake.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
what research (or clinical trials) is being done for Anencephaly?
A. Studies to understand how the brain and nervous system normally develop.
B. Studies to understand how the genetic mutations involved in neural tube disorders occur.
C. Studies to develop better treatments for seizures and other disorders of the nervous system.
D. Studies to prevent neural tube disorders.
completion:
Research supported by the NINDS includes studies to understand how the brain and nervous system normally develop. These studies contribute to a greater understanding of neural tube disorders, such as anencephaly, and open promising new avenues to treat and prevent neurological disorders.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
What are the treatments for Warthin tumor?
A. Surgery
B. Radiation therapy
C. Chemotherapy
D. Observation
completion:
Treatment of Warthin tumor generally includes surgery to remove the tumor or careful observation to watch for changes in the tumor over time. Because Warthin tumor is almost always benign, additional treatment (i.e. radiation therapy and/or chemotherapy) is rarely needed
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:

prompt:

prompt:

prompt:
How many people are affected by SOST-related sclerosing bone dysplasia?

B. 1000
C. 10000
D. 100000
completion:

prompt:
Is Costello syndrome inherited?
A. Yes
B. No
C. Maybe
D. Who knows?
completion:
Costello syndrome is considered to be an autosomal dominant condition, which means one copy of the altered gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the disorder. Almost all reported cases have resulted from new gene mutations and have occurred in people with no history of the disorder in their family.
<answer>C</answer>

prompt:

completion:

<answer>B</answer>

prompt:
Is Ochoa syndrome inherited?
A. Yes

C. Maybe
D. Maybe
completion:
This condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, which means both copies of the gene in each cell have mutations. The parents of an individual with an autosomal recessive condition each carry one copy of the mutated gene, but they typically do not show signs and symptoms of the condition.
<answer>A</answer>

prompt:
What causes Factor V deficiency?
A. It's due to a lack of vitamin C.
B. It's caused by a deficiency in vitamin K.
C. It's due to a deficiency of another clotting factor, such as Factor II or Factor VII.
D. It's caused by a mutation in the F5 gene.
completion:
Factor V deficiency is caused by mutations in the F5 gene that prevent the production of a functional factor V protein or decrease the amount of the protein in the bloodstream. Mutations are present in both copies of the F5 gene in each cell, which prevents blood from clotting normally.
<answer>D</answer>

prompt:
How many people are affected by spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia congenita?
A. 1000
B. 10000
C. 100000
D. 1000000
completion:
This condition is rare; the exact incidence is unknown. More than 175 cases have been reported in the scientific literature.
<answer>B</answer>
